extra.reasoning:
The property globally(opted_out implies not sent) HOLDS for the entire reachable state space.

The only transition that sets sent:=true while opted_out is true is t4 (on send_attempt if opted_out and acc==5). Every other path to sent:=true is t2, which requires not opted_out. So a violation would require reaching a state with opted_out=true and acc==5, then firing send_attempt.

But acc==5 is unreachable. The step updates are all of the form acc := (3*acc + k) mod 32 with k in {12,30,18}, all even (t5 k=12, t6 k=30, t7 k=18; the nested if-then-else with subtractions of 32 implements mod 32). Initial acc=0 is even. Since 3*even is even, even+even is even, and mod 32 preserves parity (32 even), acc is invariantly even. 5 is odd, so the t4 guard acc==5 is never satisfiable. Thus t4 can never fire.

While opted_out is true, the only enabled send_attempt transition is t3 (because acc!=5 always), which sets sent:=false; the step transitions also set sent:=false; and t1 (optout) sets sent:=false on the same step it sets opted_out:=true. opted_out is monotone (never reset). Hence no reachable state has opted_out and sent simultaneously true.

I confirmed this with exhaustive BFS over the full state (opted_out, sent, acc): the reachable acc values are exactly the even numbers {0,2,...,30} (16 values), the full reachable state space is 48 states, and zero states satisfy opted_out AND sent. The state space is fully explored well within the 34-event bound. Therefore the property holds and there is no counterexample.
